epigraph.text:
The Lord wraps himself in light as with a garment; he 
stretches out the heavens like a tent.

content.text:
Sometimes the disk and washer methods require awkward algebra—solving for 
$x$ in terms of $y$, or splitting a region into multiple integrals. The 
**shell method** offers an elegant alternative. Instead of slicing the 
solid into disks (perpendicular to the axis), we peel it into thin 
cylindrical shells (parallel to the axis), like the layers of an onion.

The universal pattern remains: **Slice, Approximate, Sum, Integrate.** 
Only the shape of the slice changes.

Imagine revolving the region under $y = f(x) \ge 0$ from $x = a \ge 0$ to 
$x = b$ about the $y$-axis. Instead of cutting the resulting solid into 
horizontal disks, let us peel it into thin cylindrical shells.

**Step 1: Slice.** Divide $[a,b]$ into $n$ subintervals of width 
$\Delta x$. Each subinterval at position $x_k^*$ generates a thin 
cylindrical shell when revolved about the $y$-axis.

If you "unroll" the thin cylinder, it becomes approximately a rectangular 
slab with length $2\pi r_k$ (the circumference), height $h_k$, and thickness 
$\Delta x$. Its volume is
\[
 \Delta V_k \approx 2\pi\,x_k^*\,f(x_k^*)\,\Delta x.
\]

content.title: Volume by Cylindrical Shells (Revolution about the $y$-axis)
content.content:
If $f$ is continuous and nonnegative on $[a,b]$ with $0 \le a < b$, the 
volume of the solid obtained by revolving the region under $y = f(x)$ about 
the $y$-axis is
\[
 V = 2\pi\defint{a}{b} x\,f(x)\dx.
\]
More generally, the shell formula is
\[
 V = 2\pi\defint{a}{b} (\text{radius})\times(\text{height})\dx.
\]

content.caption: The cylindrical shell method for volumes of revolution
content.alt: Diagram illustrating the shell method. On the left, a curve y = f(x) is shown above the x-axis with a thin vertical strip of width Δx at position x highlighted. On the right, the solid of revolution is shown after revolving about the y-axis. A representative cylindrical shell is highlighted, with labels showing radius r = x (distance from the y-axis), height h = f(x), and thickness Δx. An "unrolled" view shows the shell as a rectangular slab with dimensions 2πr × h × Δx.

content.title: Shells vs.\ Disks: Which Axis?
content.content:
The shell method integrates *parallel* to the axis of revolution:

- Revolution about the $y$-axis $\implies$ integrate with respect 
 to $x$ (shells in $x$).

- Revolution about the $x$-axis $\implies$ integrate with respect 
 to $y$ (shells in $y$).
This is the *opposite* of the disk/washer method, which integrates 
perpendicular to the axis.

content.title: Shell method --- basic
content.problem:
Find the volume of the solid obtained by revolving the region under 
$y = x^2$ from $x = 0$ to $x = 2$ about the $y$-axis.
content.solution:
Each shell at position $x$ has radius $x$ and height $x^2$:
$$
 V &= 2\pi\defint{0}{2} x \cdot x^2\dx = 2\pi\defint{0}{2}x^3\dx \\
 &= 2\pi\left[\frac{x^4}{4}\right]_0^2 = 2\pi\cdot 4 = 8\pi.
$$
*Check:* In Example [ex:vol-y-axis], we found $V = 8\pi$ using 
disks in $y$. The two methods agree, as they must.

content.title: When shells are clearly easier
content.problem:
Find the volume of the solid obtained by revolving the region bounded by 
$y = 2x^2 - x^3$ and $y = 0$ about the $y$-axis.
content.solution:
First, find where the curve meets $y = 0$: $2x^2 - x^3 = x^2(2-x) = 0$, 
so $x = 0$ and $x = 2$.

Using the disk/washer method would require solving $y = 2x^2 - x^3$ for 
$x$ in terms of $y$—a cubic equation. Shells avoid this entirely.

Each shell has radius $x$ and height $2x^2 - x^3$:
$$
 V &= 2\pi\defint{0}{2} x(2x^2 - x^3)\dx = 2\pi\defint{0}{2}(2x^3 - x^4)\dx \\
 &= 2\pi\left[\frac{x^4}{2} - \frac{x^5}{5}\right]_0^2 
 = 2\pi\left(8 - \frac{32}{5}\right) 
 = 2\pi\cdot\frac{8}{5} = \frac{16\pi}{5}.
$$

content.title: Shells with the region between two curves
content.problem:
Find the volume when the region between $y = x$ and $y = x^2$ 
($0 \le x \le 1$) is revolved about the $y$-axis.
content.solution:
The shell at position $x$ has radius $x$ and height 
$(\text{top}) - (\text{bottom}) = x - x^2$:
$$
 V &= 2\pi\defint{0}{1} x(x - x^2)\dx = 2\pi\defint{0}{1}(x^2 - x^3)\dx \\
 &= 2\pi\left[\frac{x^3}{3} - \frac{x^4}{4}\right]_0^1 
 = 2\pi\left(\frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{4}\right) = 2\pi\cdot\frac{1}{12} 
 = \frac{\pi}{6}.
$$

content.text:
To use shells for revolution about the $x$-axis, we integrate with respect 
to $y$. Each shell has:

items: **Height:** the horizontal extent of the region at height $y$.

content.title: Shells about the $x$-axis
content.problem:
Find the volume when the region bounded by $x = y^2$ and $x = y + 2$ 
(for $-1 \le y \le 2$) is revolved about the $x$-axis.
content.solution:
At height $y$, the shell extends from $x = y^2$ (left) to $x = y + 2$ 
(right), so the height of the shell is $(y+2) - y^2$. The radius is $|y|$.

Since the region spans $y = -1$ to $y = 2$ and crosses $y = 0$, we split:
$$
 V &= 2\pi\defint{0}{2} y\bigl[(y+2) - y^2\bigr]\dy 
 + 2\pi\defint{-1}{0} (-y)\bigl[(y+2) - y^2\bigr]\dy.
$$
For the first integral:
$$
 &2\pi\defint{0}{2}(y^2 + 2y - y^3)\dy 
 = 2\pi\left[\frac{y^3}{3} + y^2 - \frac{y^4}{4}\right]_0^2 
 = 2\pi\left(\frac{8}{3} + 4 - 4\right) = \frac{16\pi}{3}.
$$
For the second integral:
$$
 &2\pi\defint{-1}{0}(-y^2 - 2y + y^3)\dy 
 = 2\pi\left[-\frac{y^3}{3} - y^2 + \frac{y^4}{4}\right]_{-1}^{0} \\
 &= 2\pi\left[0 - \left(\frac{1}{3} - 1 + \frac{1}{4}\right)\right] 
 = 2\pi\cdot\frac{5}{12} = \frac{5\pi}{6}.
$$
Total: $V = \dfrac{16\pi}{3} + \dfrac{5\pi}{6} = \dfrac{32\pi + 5\pi}{6} = \dfrac{37\pi}{6}$.

content.text:
When revolving about the line $x = L$ (instead of the $y$-axis), the radius 
of each shell changes from $x$ to $|x - L|$.

content.title: Shells about the line $x = 3$
content.problem:
Find the volume when the region under $y = x^2$ from $x = 0$ to $x = 2$ 
is revolved about the line $x = 3$.
content.solution:
Each shell at position $x$ has radius $3 - x$ (distance from $x$ to 
the line $x = 3$) and height $x^2$:
$$
 V &= 2\pi\defint{0}{2}(3-x)\cdot x^2\dx 
 = 2\pi\defint{0}{2}(3x^2 - x^3)\dx \\
 &= 2\pi\left[x^3 - \frac{x^4}{4}\right]_0^2 
 = 2\pi(8 - 4) = 8\pi.
$$

content.title: Disks/Washers vs.\ Shells
content.content:
1. **Axis of revolution:** If you revolve about the $x$-axis:
 itemize

2. Disks/washers $\to$ integrate in $x$ (slices $\perp$ to axis).

3. Shells $\to$ integrate in $y$ (slices $\parallel$ to axis).
 itemize

4. **Ease of solving:** Can you easily express the curves in the 
 variable you need? If solving for $x$ is hard, shells (in $x$) for 
 $y$-axis revolution may be easier.

5. **Number of integrals:** Sometimes one method requires a single 
 integral while the other requires two. Choose the simpler setup.

6. **When in doubt:** Try both. They must give the same answer.

content.caption: Comparison of shell and disk methods
content.alt: Side-by-side comparison showing when to use shells versus disks for volumes of revolution. Left panel: Revolution about the y-axis using shells—vertical strips of width Δx parallel to the axis are revolved to create cylindrical shells; integrate with respect to x. Right panel: Revolution about the y-axis using disks—horizontal strips of height Δy perpendicular to the axis are revolved to create disk or washer cross-sections; integrate with respect to y. The diagram emphasizes that shells integrate parallel to the axis while disks integrate perpendicular to it.

content.title: Same problem, both methods
content.problem:
Find the volume when the region bounded by $y = \sqrt{x}$, $y = 0$, and 
$x = 4$ is revolved about the $y$-axis.
content.solution:
**Method 1: Shells (in $x$).** Radius $= x$, height $= \sqrt{x}$:
\[
 V = 2\pi\defint{0}{4} x\sqrt{x}\dx = 2\pi\defint{0}{4}x^{3/2}\dx 
 = 2\pi\left[\frac{2}{5}x^{5/2}\right]_0^4 
 = 2\pi\cdot\frac{2}{5}\cdot 32 = \frac{128\pi}{5}.
\]

**Method 2: Disks (in $y$).** The region runs from $y = 0$ to 
$y = 2$. At height $y$, the disk has outer radius $4$ and inner radius 
$y^2$ (since $x = y^2$):
\[
 V = \pi\defint{0}{2}(16 - y^4)\dy 
 = \pi\left[16y - \frac{y^5}{5}\right]_0^2 
 = \pi\left(32 - \frac{32}{5}\right) = \frac{128\pi}{5}.
\]

Both methods give $V = \dfrac{128\pi}{5}$.

content.title: Pappus of Alexandria (c.\,290--350 \textsc{ad
content.content:
)}
The shell method is intimately connected to Pappus' Theorem, which we will 
encounter in Section [sec:centers-of-mass]. Pappus, one of the last 
great geometers of antiquity, proved that the volume of a solid of revolution 
equals the cross-sectional area times the distance traveled by its centroid. 
This beautiful result, forgotten for over a millennium, was rediscovered 
during the Renaissance and remains one of the most elegant shortcuts in all 
of mathematics.

marginNotes.text:
"Like clay in the hand of the potter, so 
are you in my hand.

Oh, the depth of the riches of the wisdom 
and knowledge of God! How unsearchable his judgments, and his paths beyond 
tracing out!

marginNotes.author: Isaac Newton (1643--1727)
marginNotes.text:
If I have seen further, it is by 
standing on the shoulders of giants.

marginNotes.author: Henri Poincar\'e (1854--1912)
marginNotes.text:
It is by logic that we prove, 
but by intuition that we discover.
